title: What Documents You Need to Charter a Boat
topic: What documents you need to charter a boat
html:
Booking a day on the water is one of the simplest holiday pleasures, yet the paperwork behind it can feel surprisingly opaque. Whether you plan to rent a boat for a quiet afternoon or take command of a crewed yacht for a week, knowing which documents to bring will save you a frantic dockside search and a delayed departure. This guide walks through what owners and charter companies typically ask for, and why each item matters.

Identification and proof of who you are

Every charter begins with confirming your identity. A valid passport is the safest choice for international trips, since a national ID card or driving licence may not be accepted in every country. If you are travelling abroad, carry the original document rather than a photocopy, because many operators will not release a vessel against a printed image alone.

Expect the lead renter to be the person whose name appears on the booking and the payment card. Some companies ask all guests to provide names and dates of birth for their passenger manifest, particularly when the trip crosses into another jurisdiction or involves an overnight stay on board.

Boating qualifications and licences

This is the area that trips up most first-timers. The rules depend entirely on where you are sailing and what you intend to drive.

  • Skippered or crewed charters: if a professional captain stays at the helm, you usually need no licence at all. You simply turn up, relax, and let the crew handle navigation.
  • Bareboat charters: here you take full responsibility for the vessel, so a recognised certificate of competence is almost always required. In Europe the International Certificate of Competence (ICC) is widely accepted, while other regions recognise their own national equivalents.
  • Smaller craft and tenders: in many destinations a modest motorboat can be hired with no formal qualification, though local age limits and short safety briefings still apply.

When you hire a boat through a marketplace, the listing should state clearly whether a licence is needed. If it is unclear, ask before you pay, since arriving without the right certificate can cancel your trip outright.

The sailing CV or experience declaration

For larger sailing yachts and catamarans, a certificate on its own is sometimes not enough. Operators may request a short sailing CV, a plain summary of the boats you have handled, the waters you have sailed, and the length of trips you have completed. This is not a test designed to catch you out. It helps the owner gauge whether the vessel suits your experience, and in some cases it influences the insurance terms attached to your booking.

A clear, honest sailing CV reassures owners far more than an inflated one. Overstating your skills only raises the chance of an awkward handover or a checkout skipper riding along at your expense.

Payment, deposit and insurance paperwork

Money matters generate their own small stack of documents. Be ready with the following:

  1. A credit or debit card in the lead renter's name for the balance and the security deposit.
  2. Confirmation of any deposit insurance you have purchased, which can replace a large cash hold on your card.
  3. The signed charter agreement, which sets out the cruising area, return time, fuel policy, and what happens in the event of damage.

Read that agreement carefully. The cruising limits and late-return penalties are where most disputes arise, and a few minutes of reading protects your deposit. Anyone planning to list your boat for charter should also keep these terms tidy, as transparent paperwork attracts better renters.

Extra documents for cross-border trips

Sailing between countries adds a layer of formality. You may need a crew list stamped by the marina, proof of the vessel's registration and insurance, and occasionally a transit log for entering certain national waters. The charter company normally prepares the boat's own papers, but you should confirm they are on board before you leave the berth, since a coastguard check without them can spoil the day.

A simple checklist before you set off

To keep things calm at the dock, gather these items in advance:

  • Passport or accepted photo ID for the lead renter.
  • Any boating certificate required for the vessel and location.
  • A short sailing CV for bareboat yachts.
  • Payment card and deposit cover.
  • The signed and dated charter contract.

With the paperwork sorted, the rest of the experience is pure pleasure. Take a moment to browse boats for your destination, check each listing's stated requirements, and message the owner with any questions before you confirm. A little preparation turns a charter from a bureaucratic hurdle into the effortless escape it should be.
